Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.1: ). Some of the PDF files found here are taken from the printed copy of the corresponding syllabus, if there is one. There are also resources specific to the particular course that will be assigned or referenced within the syllabus assignments. Answer Keys or Assignment overviews for the syllabus, if applicable, are only found via the parent log-in; the student log-in may access all other syllabus resources.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.2: Listen to Vivaldi’s “Four Seasons: Spring”. You could certainly listen to the whole work, all four sections, but it would be considerably longer.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.3: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Canon in D and the Four Seasons; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.4: Listen to one of the Brandenburg Concertos.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.5: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Four Seasons and the Brandenburg Concertos; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times. Is it easier or harder to distinguish the Brandenburg Concerto from the Four Seasons than it was to distinguish the Concerto from the Canon in D?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.6: Listen to the Tocatta and Fugue in D Minor.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.7: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Tocatta and Fugue and from the Canon in D; can you tell which is which? Try this exercise several times. Is it easier or harder to distinguish between these two than it was to distinguish between the Brandenburg Concerto and the Four Seasons?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.8: Now listen to the Water Music by Handel.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.9: Have someone play a short selection from the Water Music and a short selection from the Four Seasons; can you tell which is which? How about trying to distinguish between the Brandenburg Concerto and the Water Music? Can you do it? How about between the Water Music and the Canon in D? Which are the most alike and which the most different?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.10: Listen to The Well Tempered Clavier by Bach.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.11: Listen to Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor by Beethoven.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.12: Have someone play a short selection from each of the last two pieces. Can you distinguish between them? Listen to a short selection from The Well Tempered Clavier and then from the Tocatta and Fugue in D minor. Can you distinguish between them? Which of these three pieces (Tocatta and Fugue, The Well Tempered Clavier, and the Piano Sonata no. 8) are most alike? Why?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.13: Listen to the Oboe Concerto by Bach.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.14: Have someone play a short selection from each of the four Bach pieces we have listened to (Brandenburg Concerto, Tocatta and Fugue in D Minor, Oboe Concerto, and The Well Tempered Clavier). Let him choose the order, just calling each by a number. You try to put a name to each number. Can you do it?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.15: Listen to the Christmas parts of the Messiah. If you have the libretto, it is easy to find the right parts. Otherwise, just listen to the words to tell where you are in Christ’s life. The words are in English, so you can tell when the music moves past the nativity. The Hallelujah chorus, with which nearly everyone is familiar, comes at the end of the work, after the passion and crucifixion, so we’ll save that for Easter.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.16: Listen to the String Quartet in C by Haydn.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.17: Listen to the Clarinet Quintet by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.18: Have someone in your house play a three minute section from one of the last two pieces you have listened to; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.19: Have someone in your house play a short section from The Four Seasons and then from the Haydn string quartet; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.20: Listen to the Arrival of the Queen of Sheba by Handel.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.21: Have someone in your house play a short section from the Arrival of the Queen of Sheba and then from the Water Music; can you tell which is which? Then try to distinguish among the two just mentioned and the Haydn string quartet. Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.22: Listen to the Eine Kleine Nacht Music by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.23: Listen to the Piano Sonata in C, K.545, by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.24: Listen to the Clarinet Concerto in A Major by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.25: Have someone play a short selection from each of the Mozart pieces we have listened to (Clarinet Quintet, Clarinet Concerto, Eine Kleine Nacht Music, Piano Sonata in C, K.545). Let him choose the order, just calling each by a number. You try to put a name to each number. Can you do it?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.26: Listen to the Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor by Beethoven.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.27: Try to distinguish between Mozart’s Piano Sonata in C and Beethoven’s Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor. Can you do it? How would you characterize the difference?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.29: Listen to Mozart’s Piano Concerto no. 23 in A Major.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.30: Now have someone play parts of the three piano pieces you have been listening to (Piano Sonata in C, K.545, Piano Concerto no. 23 in A Major, and Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor). Can you identify which is which? This is harder to do than in many of the previous exercises because the principle instrument is the same in all three pieces.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.31: This week have someone play the pieces from this year, in a random order, and try to identify as many of the pieces from the year as you can. Listen as long as you need to in order to identify the piece. Use the list from the front of this syllabus to help you.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.32: Try again to identify as many of the pieces from the year as you can. This time don’t use a list and see how you do.

Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on the memorization of #136-143, 153, 155, 147 & 148 in your Baltimore Catechism. Write a short essay answering the question, “How do you know Christ intended to establish a visible hierarchical Church?” A good answer will include the following points:

Jesus chose those who were to carry on His work, which means He intended it to be carried on. He even specified that this work was to embrace all nations.
The apostles understood this intention, for they chose another to take the place of the apostate Judas.
Further, in the Old Testament we see a visible hierarchical structure, and we know that the New Testament is a fulfillment of the Old Testament, so we would expect to see a similar hierarchical structure.
Additionally, the first council of the Church, the Council of Jerusalem, occurred in the lifetime of the apostles. They called this council as a means of resolving differences of opinion, and they all abided by the decisions of Peter and the council as a whole. They gave a weight to the conclusions of the council beyond an ordinary general agreement. In fact, who does St. Peter mention first in announcing the decisions of the Council? (Acts 15)

In this section Lewis begins by giving three arguments to prove something about the Moral Law. What is he trying to establish?

Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.28:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 3 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.

Men ought to be _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
Therefore, _____.

If there is a power behind the things that are, that creates them and directs them, where would we look for evidence of it?
What three names does Lewis give for the in-between view he mentions in the note?

Why is Christianity a fighting religion?
How does the injustice of the world prove that there must be a God?

Day 4: Nature journaling is important. A good scientist can notice details; he pays attention to what he is studying. When you begin to journal, decide first what your objective is:

 Are you observing plants? Animals? Birds? Flowers? Insects? Landscapes? Decide that first.

You will need: a nature journal (a blank sketchbook), a drawing pencil, a sharper, color pencils if you want to focus on details with color, binoculars if you are watching birds. You may choose to use a camera to document what you see. A field guide book for your area may be helpful. If you are studying plants, a crayon to do “leaf rubs” is a good addition.

 Always note the location, time and day in your journal for each entry. Note your observations next to sketches. If you take pictures, you can paste them in the journal next to your notes.

Day 1: If you do not have a timeline already, now is the time to construct one. (Note that there are many relevant dates for your timeline listed in Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum.) Please see the resources attached to this syllabus, especially the Timeline for History Example and Timeline for History for more detailed instructions. 

Note: Please take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of
Day 2: Finish Timeline.
Day 3: Locate a good map of Mediterranean Area. The maps on p. 6 and pp. 10-11 of Historical Atlas of the World by Rand McNally are good maps for this exercise. Locate Assyria, Egypt, Greece, & Phoenicia. Think about where these countries are in relation to each other and how someone would have to travel to go from one to the next. Then locate a good map of Greece. The "Outline Map of Greece" and the "Color Map of Greece" in your syllabus resources were developed for this project. Make sure your map includes the nearby islands and the western edge of modern Turkey. (Note: The map on p. 7 of Historical Atlas of the World by Rand McNally, The Greeks, p. 81, Old World and America p. 31 also have good maps of Ancient Greece. Trace (or draw freehand) the outline of Greece and the surrounding area. Photocopy it 7 times.
Day 4: Read pp. 25-35 in Old World and America. Do the Objective Tests, and when you give the answer, also give the page number on which the answer is found.

Day 1: Read pp. 10-21 (The Mycenaeans, Mycenaean Cities, The Trojan War, The End of the Age, The Dark Ages, The Archaic Period, The Greeks and Their Neighbors, The Social Structure and Government) in The Greeks. Enter relevant information on your timeline.

Augustus Caesar's World has a passage that casts doubt on the historicity of the Gospels. We encourage parents to read this Paragraph 19 of Verbum Dei with their students to clarify the truth of this point:

"Holy Mother Church has firmly and with absolute constancy held, and continues to hold, that the four Gospels just named, whose historical character the Church unhesitatingly asserts, faithfully hand on what Jesus Christ, while living among men, really did and taught for their eternal salvation until the day He was taken up into heaven (see Acts 1:1). Indeed, after the Ascension of the Lord the Apostles handed on to their hearers what He had said and done. This they did with that clearer understanding which they enjoyed (3) after they had been instructed by the glorious events of Christ's life and taught by the light of the Spirit of truth. (2) The sacred authors wrote the four Gospels, selecting some things from the many which had been handed on by word of mouth or in writing, reducing some of them to a synthesis, explaining some things in view of the situation of their churches and preserving the form of proclamation but always in such fashion that they told us the honest truth about Jesus.(4) For their intention in writing was that either from their own memory and recollections, or from the witness of those who "themselves from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the Word" we might know "the truth" concerning those matters about which we have been instructed (see Luke 1:2-4)."

Day 3: The emphasis this year is on becoming well acquainted with a small number of great and easily accessible works of sacred music. A secondary goal will be to further the student’s understanding of music theory and basic music skills. Seventh Grade music builds upon the previous years. If you have not done Fifth or Sixth Grade Music you may want to add in Lessons 1-12 of music theory to learn previous concepts (though some review is always provided each year). Students entering in 7th Grade without previous musical background, may want to consider doing the Sixth Grade music. (If you would like to hear an excellent talk on the virtues of classical music, get Andrew Pudewa’s talk “Profound Effect of Music on Life”. This can be found here: https://iew.com/the-profound-effects-of-music-on-life-andrew-pudewa.)

The classical pieces assigned in the syllabus are favorites of my family. They can be found in a variety of places, such as YouTube, Amazon, Apple Music, Pandora, Classical Archives, and so forth. You may be able to subscribe for unlimited music or purchase selected songs. Please be aware that free sites or sites with radio stations (like Pandora) will occasionally display advertisements of questionable moral content; parents are encouraged to supervise their children’s use, as with any website.

Religion 7 (Acts of the Apostles) Syllab.5:
Day 1: You will be reading and discussing Chapters 1 and 2 of Acts this week. The questions for discussion are in the syllabus resources (here). Today read Chapter 1. Also, start working on two new Friendly Defender cards. Pick one card from each of the following categories: Confession and Church. Read the cards and think about the issues. Start to memorize the verses.
Day 2: Discuss the questions for Chapter 1. Or, if you prefer, you may write out your answers.

What is the full title of Acts?
The book begins right after an important event, or series of events. What is it? or What are they?
Jesus gave the apostles a final instruction. What did He say? And what happened next?
To what city do the apostles go after the Ascension?
How was Matthias chosen to be an apostle? Whose place did he take?
Why does Peter conduct the election?
Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review one card from last week.
Day 3: Read Chapter 2 of Acts. Also, review your cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Review one card from last week.
Day 4: Discuss the questions for Chapter 2, or write out your answers.

Which came first, Pentecost or the Ascension?
Describe the events of the coming of the Holy Spirit. They were together in one place and…(1, 2, 3, 4)
People heard about this amazing event and gathered together. What surprised them?
Who first said, “And it shall come to pass in the last days (saith the Lord), I will pour out my Spirit upon all flesh…”? What was he talking about?
Who first said, “Thou [God] wilt not leave my soul in hell, nor suffer they Holy One to see corruption…”?
To whom did he refer?
Why did Peter tell the people that Joel and David were talking about Christ?
What did Peter tell the Jews that they should do?
How many were converted that day?
What signs of charity and divine favor attended the first Christian community?
Practice defending your faith. Have someone ask you one or more of the questions you have been working on in your Friendly Defender cards. Answer the question and give the Bible verse that pertains to that answer.

Phonics & Reading K.1: Review letter sounds A–E; read short CVC words (cat, bed, pig).
Phonics & Reading K.2: Review letter sounds F–J; practice blending three-letter words.
Phonics & Reading K.3: Review letter sounds K–O; read a short pattern book together.
Phonics & Reading K.4: Review letter sounds P–T; introduce sight words 'the' and 'and'.
Phonics & Reading K.5: Review letter sounds U–Z; practice sight words 'I', 'a', 'see'.
Phonics & Reading K.6: Read short CVC words with short-A; identify rhyming pairs.
Phonics & Reading K.7: Read short CVC words with short-E; copy two sight words neatly.
Phonics & Reading K.8: Read short CVC words with short-I; reread a familiar pattern book.
Phonics & Reading K.9: Read short CVC words with short-O; introduce sight word 'is'.
Phonics & Reading K.10: Read short CVC words with short-U; review all five short vowels.
Phonics & Reading K.15: Mixed digraph review; read a short story aloud.
Phonics & Reading K.16: Long vowel A (silent-e): cake, lake, gate.
Phonics & Reading K.17: Long vowel I (silent-e): bike, ride, kite.
Phonics & Reading K.18: Long vowel O (silent-e): bone, home, rope.
Phonics & Reading K.19: Long vowel U (silent-e): cube, tube, mule.
Phonics & Reading K.20: Mixed long-vowel review; read a short Bob Book.
Phonics & Reading K.21: Beginning blends bl, br: blue, brown.
Phonics & Reading K.22: Beginning blends cl, cr: clap, crab.
Phonics & Reading K.23: Beginning blends fl, fr: flag, frog.
Phonics & Reading K.24: Beginning blends gl, gr, pl, pr: glad, grin, plan, prim.
Phonics & Reading K.25: Beginning blends sl, sm, sn, sp, st, sw: slip, smell, snap.
Phonics & Reading K.30: R-controlled vowels ar, or: car, fork.
Phonics & Reading K.31: Read a level-A or B early reader independently.
Phonics & Reading K.32: End-of-year review: read aloud and retell a short story.

Math K (Saxon Math K).1: Count to 20 forward; identify written numerals 0–10.
Math K (Saxon Math K).2: Count to 30; sort objects by color.
Math K (Saxon Math K).3: Count to 40; identify shapes: circle, square, triangle.
Math K (Saxon Math K).4: Count to 50; copy a simple AB pattern with manipulatives.
Math K (Saxon Math K).5: Count by 1s to 60; compare two groups (more, fewer, same).
Math K (Saxon Math K).6: Count by 1s to 70; introduce written numerals 11–15.
Math K (Saxon Math K).7: Count by 1s to 80; introduce written numerals 16–20.
Math K (Saxon Math K).8: Count by 10s to 100; practice writing numerals 0–9.
Math K (Saxon Math K).10: Introduce addition concept with manipulatives (sums to 5).
Math K (Saxon Math K).11: Addition facts to 5 with counters; write the equations.
Math K (Saxon Math K).12: Addition facts to 10 with counters; oral practice.
Math K (Saxon Math K).13: Days of the week and months of the year orally.
Math K (Saxon Math K).14: Tell time to the hour on an analog clock.
Math K (Saxon Math K).15: Identify pennies, nickels, dimes; count by 1s and 5s.
Math K (Saxon Math K).16: Compare lengths: longer / shorter / about the same.
Math K (Saxon Math K).17: Compare weights: heavier / lighter using a balance.
Math K (Saxon Math K).25: Introduce greater than / less than with two numbers under 10.
Math K (Saxon Math K).26: Identify halves and quarters with paper folding.
Math K (Saxon Math K).27: Read a simple bar graph with 3 categories.
Math K (Saxon Math K).28: Word problems: combine two small groups (sums to 10).
Math K (Saxon Math K).29: Word problems: take away from a small group.
Math K (Saxon Math K).30: Money: count combinations of pennies and nickels to 25¢.
Math K (Saxon Math K).31: Time: identify the half hour on an analog clock.
Math K (Saxon Math K).32: End-of-year review: count to 100, write 0–20, simple sums.

Religion K (Faith and Life K).1: God made everything — discuss creation with picture book.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).2: God made me — child draws a self-portrait and names a gift God gave him.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).3: Make the Sign of the Cross slowly together; practice three times.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).4: Memorize: 'Glory be to the Father' (first half).
Religion K (Faith and Life K).5: Memorize: 'Glory be to the Father' (second half); recite whole prayer.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).6: Story of Adam and Eve in the garden — read and discuss.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).8: Memorize: 'Hail Mary' (second half); recite whole prayer.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).9: Story of Noah's Ark — read and discuss God's promise.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).10: Memorize: 'Our Father' (first phrase: 'Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be thy name').
Religion K (Faith and Life K).11: Memorize: 'Our Father' (next phrase: 'Thy kingdom come, thy will be done').
Religion K (Faith and Life K).12: Memorize: 'Our Father' (next phrase: 'Give us this day our daily bread').
Religion K (Faith and Life K).13: Memorize: 'Our Father' (final phrase); recite whole prayer.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).14: Story of Abraham — God's friend who trusted Him.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).15: Story of Moses and the burning bush.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).16: The Ten Commandments — name the first three with Mom.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).19: Advent / Christmas: the Annunciation — read Luke 1 simply.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).20: Christmas: the Nativity — talk about the manger.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).22: The Holy Family — Joseph, Mary, and Jesus.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).23: Jesus is baptized by John in the Jordan.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).24: Jesus calls the first apostles; name a few.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).25: Jesus heals the sick — pick one Gospel story to read.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).27: Lent: Jesus prays and fasts in the desert 40 days.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).28: Holy Week: Palm Sunday — read briefly.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).30: Mary, our Mother — pray a decade of the Rosary together.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).31: Saints: pick one favorite saint and tell his story.
Religion K (Faith and Life K).32: End-of-year review: recite Sign of the Cross, Glory Be, Hail Mary, Our Father.

Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.1: Practice letters L and F (vertical and horizontal lines).
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.14: Review all uppercase letters; write own first name.
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.19: Practice lowercase l, k, y, j.
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.20: Practice lowercase p, r, n, m.
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.23: Write own first and last name.
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.24: Copy three short words: cat, dog, sun.
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.25: Copy a short sentence: 'I see a cat.'
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.26: Copy a short sentence: 'The sun is up.'
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.27: Copy a short sentence: 'God is good.'
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.30: Write the days of the week (with help).
Handwriting K (Handwriting Without Tears.32: End-of-year review: write own name, address, and one short sentence.

Art K.1: Crayon free draw — name three colors used.
Art K.2: Color in a picture of a saint; stay inside the lines.
Art K.3: Cut and paste shapes to make a house.
Art K.4: Finger paint — explore primary colors red, yellow, blue.
Art K.5: Mix red + yellow → orange; paint a pumpkin.
Art K.6: Mix blue + yellow → green; paint grass and a tree.
Art K.7: Mix red + blue → purple; paint flowers.
Art K.8: Draw your family — label each person.
Art K.9: Draw your home — include a door, two windows, a roof.
Art K.10: Watercolor wash — practice loading the brush with water.
Art K.12: Make a leaf rubbing with crayon.
Art K.13: Trace your own hand and decorate it.
Art K.14: Cut a paper snowflake from a folded square.
Art K.15: Make a Christmas card for a grandparent.
Art K.17: Make a paper crown for the Wise Men.
Art K.18: Tear-paper collage of a winter tree.
Art K.19: Draw and color a winter snow scene.
Art K.20: Modeling clay — make a coiled snake.
Art K.21: Modeling clay — make a small bowl by pinching.
Art K.22: Draw a self-portrait with details (eyes, nose, mouth, hair).
Art K.24: Make an Easter card for Mom or Dad.
Art K.26: Draw your favorite Bible story scene.
Art K.27: Print with sliced fruits or vegetables.
Art K.30: Outdoor sketch — draw something you see in the yard.
Art K.32: End-of-year review: pick a favorite project and tell Mom about it.

Day 1: Begin with the poem “Rain” by Robert Louis Stevenson. It is short and cheerful and easy to memorize. Read the poem aloud to your child. Ask him to repeat the title and author a few times. If he likes, read it again. (This poem and all those that follow can be found in The Harp and Laurel Wreath.)

Note: Please take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of
Day 2: Ask your child if he remembers the name of the poem that you read yesterday. The author? If not, tell him again and ask him to repeat it.  Read the poem aloud. Repeat the first line, “The rain is raining all around,” and ask him to say that. Then ask him to say the title, author and first line.  Help him if necessary. Let him repeat it until he is comfortable with it.  Read the whole poem to him one more time.
Day 3: Ask your child to tell you the name of the poem he is learning, and the author.  Can he say the first line?  Help him if necessary.  Now read the whole poem aloud.  Repeat the title and author and the first two lines, e.g

“‘Rain’ by Robert Louis Stevenson,
The rain is raining all around,
It falls on field and tree,”
Ask him to say it with you, or, if he can, to say it alone. Say it a few times together. Then ask him if he remembers what else it “rains on.” If he knows have him repeat the next line, “It rains on the umbrellas here,” correcting any mistakes.  If he can’t remember, tell him.
Day 4: Have your child repeat all that he can of the poem. Read the poem aloud.  Repeat the first three lines, then ask him to repeat them. Now say the fourth line, “And on the ships at sea.” Ask him to repeat just the last two lines. Now ask him to try to say the whole poem. Help him when he gets stuck. Read the poem one more time.
Poetry K Syllabus.2:
Day 1: Read the whole of “Rain” aloud. Ask your child to say lines 1-2. Tell him what the next line says. Ask him to repeat that line. When he has, ask him to say the first three lines and now add the new line. Ask him to repeat it a few times, help him if he gets stuck.
Day 2: Say all 4 lines of the poem together. Now have your child say them alone.  Ask him to repeat these a few times. Say the whole poem for him. Ask him to see if he can, too. Let him try a few times. As soon as he says it, congratulate him and stop, even if it isn’t perfectly smooth.
Day 3: Say the whole poem together. Now ask your child to say it alone. Be willing to help if necessary. Ask him to say it again.
Day 4: Have your child say the whole poem alone. Give him time to correct any mistakes himself before you make corrections. Unless he knows it perfectly, ask him to say it again.
Poetry K Syllabus.3:
Day 1: Have your child recite the whole poem, “Rain”, including the title and author.
Day 2: Ask your child to recite the whole poem again.  While he says it, copy it into his “Poetry Book”.  This can be an artist’s sketch book, or blank book without lines.  Leave room for a picture on the same page or on the adjacent page.
Day 3: After your child recites his whole poem, let him illustrate it in his Poetry Book.
Day 4: Ask the child to recite his poem one last time.  If he is willing, ask him to say it for another person, a brother or sister or grandparent, for example.  From now on you may want to use dinner parties or visits by friends and family as occasions for your child to recite his poems.  He may be reticent at first, but after a while he will become more accustomed to reciting for other people.
Poetry K Syllabus.4:
Day 1: Begin “Bed in Summer” by Robert Louis Stevenson.  Listen to it and repeat title and author.
Day 2: Remember the title and author of the new poem?  Listen to it again. Listen to 1st stanza again.
Day 3: Say title and author.  Listen to whole poem.  Listen to 1st stanza.  Repeat first 2 lines.
Day 4: Say the title, author and first 2 lines. Also review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.5:
Day 1: Recite title, author and 2 lines.  Listen to whole poem.  Listen to 1st stanza again.  Repeat the 1st stanza with help, and then alone.
Day 2: 1st stanza.
Day 3: 1st stanza.  Begin 2nd stanza.
Day 4: 1st stanza.  Listen to and try to repeat 2nd stanza. Also review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.6:
Day 1: Repeat title, author and 1st stanza.  Listen to 2nd stanza. Recite what you can.
Day 2: 1st stanza and next 2 lines.
Day 3: 1st stanza and next 2 lines. Repeat the whole 2nd stanza with help.
Day 4: 2 stanzas. Also review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.7:
Day 1: Recite the title, author, and 2 stanzas. Listen to 3rd stanza.
Day 2: Title, author, and 2 stanzas.  Add two more lines.
Day 3: Recite 2 stanzas plus 2 more lines.
Day 4: Recite what you already know. Listen to whole poem.  Listen to last two lines again.  Repeat last 2 lines.  Repeat 3rd stanza. Also review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.8:
Day 1: Recite title, author, 3 stanzas, with help.  Repeat.
Day 2: Recite poem (with help if needed).
Day 3: Recite poem.  Illustrate in Poetry Book as you did with the last poem.
Day 4: Recite poem.  Have it copied into Poetry Book. Also review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.9:
Day 1: Begin “The Cow” by Robert Louis Stevenson. Listen to it a couple of times. Review “Bed in Summer”.
Day 2: Listen to poem again. Repeat title, author and try to say first two lines.
Day 3: Listen to whole poem. Repeat 2 lines. Listen to first stanza. Repeat first stanza.
Day 4: 1st stanza, first with help, finally without help. Review “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.10:
Day 1: Recite title, author and 1st stanza of “The Cow”.  Listen to 2nd stanza. Repeat as much as you can. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 2: Recite 1st stanza and part of 2nd stanza.  Listen to all of 2nd stanza.  Repeat.
Day 3: Recite 1st and 2nd stanzas.  Listen to 1st and 2nd stanzas. Recite again.
Day 4: Recite 1st and 2nd stanzas.  Listen to 3rd stanza.  Try to repeat.  Repeat all so far. Also recite “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.11:
Day 1: 1st, 2nd and part of 3rd stanza. Listen to 3rd. Repeat 2 lines of 3rd. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 2: Say the 1st, 2nd and 3rd stanzas with help.  Repeat without help.
Day 3: Recite whole poem.  Listen to whole poem.  Recite again.
Day 4: Recite whole poem. Also recite “Rain”.
Poetry K Syllabus.12:
Day 1: Recite “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite whole poem while the teacher copies it into your Poetry Book. Also recite “Rain”.
Day 3: Recite and enjoy your poem.
Day 4: Recite and enjoy your poem. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Poetry K Syllabus.13:
Day 1: Listen to “The Moon” by Robert Louis Stevenson. Also recite “Rain”, and “Bed in Summer”.
Day 2: Listen to whole poem.  Repeat title, author, and 2 lines. Also recite “The Cow”.
Day 3: Listen to whole poem.  Repeat title, author, and 2 lines.
Day 4: Recite 2 lines.  Begin to learn 3rd and 4th lines. Also recite your favorite poem.
Poetry K Syllabus.14:
Day 1: Listen to whole poem. Repeat first 4 lines Also recite “The Cow”.
Day 2: Listen to whole poem. Repeat 4 lines. Listen again. Repeat second stanza. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Listen to whole poem. Recite second stanza. Review first stanza. Also recite “Rain”.
Day 4: Recite up to “. . . the light of the moon.” Also recite your favorite poem.
Poetry K Syllabus.15:
Day 1: Recite 8 lines. Listen to whole poem. Repeat third stanza, line by line. Also recite “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite lines 1-8. Work on third stanza. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite whole poem, with help if needed.  Also recite “Rain”.
Day 4: Recite whole poem and illustrate in Poetry Book. Recite all your poems.
Poetry K Syllabus.16:
Day 1: Listen to the poem “At the Seaside” by Robert Louis Stevenson. Also recite “Rain” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Listen to the poem.  Repeat 2 lines. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite title, author and 2 lines. Listen to the 3rd line.  Try to repeat it. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 4: Recite lines 1-3. Also recite “The Moon”.
Poetry K Syllabus.17:
Day 1: Listen to whole poem “At the Seaside”.  Recite the first 3 lines.  Now try to repeat the 4th. Also recite “Rain” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite lines 1-4.  Listen to the next line.  Try to repeat it alone.  Now try to recite lines 1-5. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite lines 1-5.  Listen to the last line, “Till it could come no more.”  Try to memorize this line. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 4: Recite the last line. Now try to say the whole poem. Recite all your poems.
Poetry K Syllabus.18:
Day 1: Recite your whole poem “At the Seaside”,with help if you need it. Also recite “Rain” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite poem while it is entered in your Poetry Book. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite and enjoy your poem. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 4: Recite your poem.  Review all poems previously learned.
Poetry K Syllabus.19:
Day 1: Begin “My Shadow” by Robert Louis Stevenson.  Listen to it a couple of times. Also recite “Rain” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Do you remember the title and author of the new poem?  Repeat them. Also recite “Bed in Summer” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 3: Title, author and first line. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 4: Recite 1st line, begin 2nd. Also recite your favorite poem.
Poetry K Syllabus.20:
Day 1: Recite 2 lines of “My Shadow”. Listen to next line. Repeat it. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 2: Recite 3 lines.  Listen to next line. Repeat it. Also recite “Bed in Summer” and “The Cow”.
Day 3: Recite 3 lines. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 4: Listen to 1st stanza.  Try to recite the whole stanza. Also pick two poems to recite.
Poetry K Syllabus.21:
Day 1: Recite 1st stanza.  Listen to 2nd stanza. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Recite 1st stanza plus one more line. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite 1st stanza plus two more lines. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite 6 lines. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.22:
Day 1: Recite title, author and 6 lines.  Listen to line 7. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Recite 7 lines.  Listen to next line.  Try to repeat it. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite 8 lines (2 stanzas), with help if needed. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite 2 stanzas. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.23:
Day 1: Recite 2 stanzas. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Listen to 3rd stanza. Recite 2 stanzas.  Try to add 2 lines of 3rd.  Repeat. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite 2 lines of 3rd stanza. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite 2 ½ stanzas. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.24:
Day 1: Recite title, author and 2 ½ stanzas. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Listen to all of 3rd stanza.  Repeat last 2 lines until smooth. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Repeat 3rd stanza until smooth. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite stanzas 1, 2 & 3. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.25:
Day 1: Recite 3 stanzas.  Listen to 4th stanza.  Repeat 2 lines of 4th stanza. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Recite 3 stanzas.  Listen to 4th. Repeat 3rd stanza plus 2 lines of 4th. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite 3 ½ stanzas, with help first and then try it alone. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite 3 ½ stanzas. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.26:
Day 1: Recite 3 ½ stanzas. Listen to 4th.  Repeat. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Recite 3 ½ stanzas.  Try to recite end of 4th.  Try again. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Recite whole poem with help. Again. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Recite whole poem. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.27:
Day 1: Recite whole poem. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 2: Recite your whole poem while it is copied into your Poetry Book. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 3: Listen to “The Little Turtle” by Vachel Lindsay. Also recite “Rain” and “At the Seaside”.
Day 4: Listen to “The Little Turtle” again.  Repeat the title and author. Also recite “The Cow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.28:
Day 1: Recite title and author of new poem. Listen to whole poem. Repeat first 2 lines. Also recite “Rain”, “At the Seaside” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite title and author. Listen to whole poem. Repeat first 2 lines. Again. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 3: Recite 2 lines.  Learn 2 more.  Repeat all 4 lines a couple of times. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 4: Recite 4 lines. Listen to the next 2. Try to recite all 6 lines, first with help, then alone. Also recite “My Shadow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.29:
Day 1: Recite title, author and 6 lines. Listen to the whole poem. Now listen to lines 7 and 8. Try to repeat them. Add them to the first 6 lines. Also recite “Rain”, “At the Seaside” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite 8 lines with help.  Recite them alone. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 3: Recite 8 lines.  Listen to the next 2 lines.  Try to repeat them. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 4: Recite 8 lines.  Add more if you can. Also recite “My Shadow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.30:
Day 1: Recite 10 lines.  Listen to next 2 lines. Also recite “Rain”, “At the Seaside” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite 10 lines.  Listen to next 2 lines.  Repeat 12 lines. Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 3: Recite 12 lines, with help first.  Now alone. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 4: Recite whole poem. Also recite “My Shadow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.31:
Day 1: Listen to whole poem.  Recite it. Also recite “Rain”, “At the Seaside” and “The Cow”.
Day 2: Recite all of “The Little Turtle”.  Also recite “The Moon”.
Day 3: Recite whole poem. Also recite “Bed in Summer”.
Day 4: Recite and enjoy your poem. Also recite “My Shadow”.
Poetry K Syllabus.32:
Day 1: Recite “The Little Turtle”.  Have it copied into your Poetry Book. Also pick two other poems to recite.
Day 2: Recite your poem.  Review two of the remaining poems.
Day 3: Recite whole poem and review two of the remaining poems.
Day 4: Recite and enjoy all of your poems. Congratulations!
